Portfolio Review

Equities retreated from their postelection surge. Non-U.S. developed markets stocks finished the fourth quarter lower. After jumping higher on the U.S. election results in November, equities pulled back as investors paused to consider the implications for global markets and the potential for a slower pace of easing by central banks. There was a negligible difference in performance between growth and value.

Growth opportunities have narrowed. Political uncertainty in some of the largest European economies has diminished their growth outlook. Companies with more U.S. exposure seem better positioned as the new administration is expected to be supportive. Japan is another bright spot as efforts to improve corporate governance, structure and efficiency continue to be a major focus in the country.

Positioning among financials was a source of weakness. Banking industry names were the primary detractors in the sector compared with the benchmark. Nu Holdings (NU) weighed on relative performance as investors considered the company’s decelerating growth from outsize levels. The portfolio’s lack of exposure to performing benchmark names in the banking and insurance industries also hurt.

Consumer staples holdings weighed on relative returns. Within the consumer staples distribution and retail industry, Japanese supermarket operator Kobe Bussan (OTCPK:KOBNF)(OTCPK:KOBCY) was the primary detractor compared with the benchmark as investors worried about the short-term impact of fluctuations in the yen. Italian beverage company Davide Campari-Milano (OTCPK:DVDCF)(OTCPK:DVCMY)also notably weighed on relative performance after it reported disappointing results and the company’s CEO abruptly departed.

Information technology holdings added to performance. Within the IT services industry, Shopify (SHOP) was a key contributor compared with the benchmark after the company announced solid quarterly results and a new artificial intelligence integration. Enterprise software company SAP (SAP) gave a boost to relative returns. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TSM) also contributed notably as investors showed enthusiasm for the company’s high-end chips.

Key Contributors

Shopify. This leading global e-commerce platform and payment service provider reported better-than-expected revenue growth and accelerating monthly recurring revenue. The company also launched Shopify Inbox, an artificial intelligence(AI)-enabled tool that automatically generates tailored responses to customer inquiries.

SAP. This enterprise software company’s earnings are benefiting from demand for its cloud and AI offerings. Company restructuring initiatives have also helped.

Barclays (BCS)(OTCPK:BCLYF). Shares of the bank performed well after it reported a solid set of results. Domestic net interest income and investment banking revenues improved. The stock has rerated higher, but we believe it remains inexpensive relative to tangible book value and forward earnings.

Key Detractors

Novo Nordisk (NVO)(OTCPK:NONOF). Shares of the Denmark-based drugmaker declined after a late-stage trial for the company’s new CagriSema obesity treatment showed lower-than-expected weight-loss results.

Toyota Motor (TM)(OTCPK:TOYOF). The portfolio does not hold shares of Toyota. Given the stock’s position in the benchmark and its returns for the period, the lack of exposure was a drag on relative performance.

Kobe Bussan. The Japanese supermarket operator's stock traded lower after it reported lower-than-expected operating profits. We believe long-term growth remains supported by new store openings and expansion into new product categories and private labels.

Notable Trades

MonotaRO (OTCPK:MONOY)(OTCPK:MONOF). We initiated a position in this Japanese e-commerce industrial supplier, expecting revenue growth will accelerate as its customer account growth recovers from a period of depressed growth following a pandemic-period spike. Daily new account growth inflected positively in August for the first time since 2021.

Societe Generale (OTCPK:SCGLY)(OTCPK:SCGLF). We initiated a position in this France-based banking and financial services firm as we believe its net interest income is poised to inflect. The company’s net interest margins have improved with an aggressive cost savings program and a repricing of its mortgage holdings.

Sika (OTCPK:SXYAY)(OTCPK:SKFOF). We exited the position as key European and Chinese construction markets remain depressed with low visibility of recovery. Gross margin is at a near-term high due to pricing and raw materials cost. Without a strong recovery in demand, the normalization of gross margin will continue to pressure earnings growth in 2025.

ICON. We sold the position in this provider of outsourced clinical trial and commercialization services for the pharmaceuticals industry due to concerns of near-term decelerating earnings growth trends. Biotechnology funding has weakened, and ICON’s clients continue to restructure and cut their research and development budgets.

Portfolio Positioning

The portfolio continues to invest in companies we believe are strong and improving but whose share price performance does not fully reflect these factors. Our process is based on individual security selection, but broad themes have emerged.

Energy infrastructure demand is a strong secular force. As the world moves to rely less on fossil fuels, artificial intelligence(AI) investment is leading to a significant increase in demand for new power generation. We are finding growth opportunities related to data center build-outs and large-scale energy infrastructure projects.

Pharmaceutical innovation creates opportunities in health care. Innovative companies in the pharmaceuticals industry focused on very specific therapeutic areas with unmet needs are positioned to benefit from an inflection in growth. Obesity, cancer and skin conditions are areas that continue to generate a tremendous amount of investment opportunity.

Digital transformation supports information technology positions. The acceleration of digitalization is benefiting technology holdings exposed to cloud computing, automation, digital payments and information technology services growth. Japanese companies, in particular, are investing heavily to increase efficiency and profits as they seek to catch up with their international competitors.

AI integration enables growth. Data-centric companies across sectors are integrating AI tools to drive incremental growth through new and improved product and service offerings.

Ingredients makers benefit from volume growth. Easing inflation pressures and the completion of the destocking cycle have led to an acceleration of volumes among consumer companies. Small, specialized ingredients makers for food, fragrances and personal care products add value through innovation, allowing these companies to outgrow the volumes of finished goods.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: GameStop Raises Eyebrows With International Store Sales
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify.

GME raises eyebrows with sales of assets in Canada, France. (0:15) Super Micro Computer shares up 100% from recent low. (2:00) Intel pops on talk of TSM-Broadcom deal. (3:17)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast.

Our top story so far. GameStop (NYSE:GME) announced that it intends to pursue a sale of its operations in France and Canada. The retailer said the decision was part of its evaluation of its international assets.

The development is interesting after GameStop (GME) rallied last week amid buzz over a possible entry back into the crypto sector. CNBC reported that the retailer is eyeing investments in alternative asset classes, including crypto and especially bitcoin (BTC-USD).

Sources indicated that GameStop is still figuring out if the investment is sensible for the business. The report caught the attention of investors because GME had $4.6 billion in cash holdings at the end of Q3, compared to its current market cap of $12.1 billion.

The company has a mixed history with cryptocurrency and blockchain initiatives in general. It launched a self-custodial cryptocurrency wallet in 2022 that allowed customers to manage digital assets, including cryptocurrencies and non-fungible tokens. The move was part of GameStop's broader strategy to expand into the digital asset space.

On the economic front, The Empire State Manufacturing Index bounced up to +5.7 in February, moving to expansion territory from -12.6 in January. Economists were looking for a smaller rise to -0.5. New orders and shipments increased moderately, delivery times grew slightly longer and supply availability was slightly lower.

But Pantheon Macro economist Oliver Allen expressed some concern with the prices paid index jumping to a two-year-high, with prices received also jumping.

"That might be a sign that the tariff-linked surge in demand for durable goods since late last year is starting to put some upward pressure on prices, although neither index has yet strayed far from its typical pre-Covid range, and most other leading indicators of core goods prices remain relatively benign," he said.

Among active stocks, Super Micro Computer (SMCI) is rallying again, exactly one week before its February 25 deadline to file its 2024 annual report, along with its quarterly reports for the September 2024 and December 2024 periods.

The stock is up about 100% from its recent February low before it releases preliminary earnings. And it’s up 200% since the 52-week low hit in December.

Medtronic (MDT) is the biggest S&P decliner after it only reaffirmed its full-year outlook despite a major opportunity to consolidate its lead in the market for pulsed field ablation systems following a setback to rival J&J (JNJ).

JNJ announced a temporary pause in its limited commercial rollout of the Varipulse Pulsed Field Ablation Platform, which was approved by the FDA just a few weeks ago. While the company decided to resume the rollout on Friday after an investigation found no safety concerns, the damage was already done in its ability to gain an early lead in the PFA market.

But Medtronic said it continues to expect its FY25 organic revenue growth to reach 4.75% - 5% and its diluted non-GAAP EPS to stand at $5.44-$5.50.

And shares of Constellation Brands (STZ) are rallying after Warren Buffett’s Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) took a new position in the stock in Q4 valued at $1.24 billion.

In other news of note, the sell side weighs in as reports swirl that Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) and Broadcom (AVGO) could each look to acquire parts of Intel (INTC).

BofA analyst Vivek Arya said a scenario where Broadcom acquires Intel's chip design and marketing business and Taiwan Semi takes over the manufacturing business would be “time-consuming” and “complicated.” There would be “extensive” approval requirements from global regulators, given Intel's dominant market share in the PC and server CPU spaces.

Other issues include Broadcom's $58 billion in net debt on its balance sheet and constraints provided by the CHIPs Act on Intel, as it is required to own 50% or more of its manufacturing capabilities.

J.P. Morgan credit analyst Christian Crosby said the “ideal” outcome is for Intel to separate its foundry unit into a new company, which would include Taiwan Semiconductor taking a minority voting stake and operating responsibilities of the combined unit.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, BofA’s February Global Fund Manager Survey shows that 89% of respondents see U.S. stocks as overvalued, the most since at least April 2001.

In addition, the U.S. exceptionalism trade looks to have peaked, becoming slightly less crowded in February.

The trade "has been characterized by a combo of strong US dollar (DXY) and an equity bull market led by the 'Magnificent 7' group of stocks," strategist Michael Hartnett said.

This month, long U.S. exceptionalism "was viewed as the most crowded trade by 73% of FMS investors, a decline from the three-year high of 80% reached in January."

But while they may be wary of U.S. stocks, money managers are putting cash to work in Europe and bond-sensitive assets, with cash levels of 3.5% at a 15-year low.

data.title: SL Green: On To Fiscal Year 2025
data.description:
SL Green Realty Corp. (NYSE:SLG) just completed a blockbuster fiscal year 2024. The last article spent some time on the fiscal year 2025 plans. As seems to be a usual pattern for the beginning of the fiscal year, Mr. Market has the usual doubts about the plan. Then again, the market revised those doubts about the last fiscal year plan as the year unfolded, which resulted in a very good year for shareholders. As the office part of the REIT industry continues to largely struggle, this office REIT is proving to be quite an exception to the trend. It may take a while for Mr. Market to realize that this REIT will not be joining the industry trend unless there is a recession or worse.

Debt

Debt levels are extremely low for this outperforming REIT.

One of the signs of superior management is the ability to outperform the company's sector of the REIT part of the industry while using less leverage in the process. Many investors feel that financial leverage is how above-average returns are achieved. But many managements I follow believe in low debt levels while finding superior profit returns. This difference in views has been around for as long as I can remember.

Interestingly, those managements, like this one, that can produce good results with lower debt often provide a superior long-term return. Even given that, there is still an investor migration towards higher debt level companies.

FFO

Now management reported a big increase in funds from operations, or FFO. Less than half of that increase came from settling some debt for less than the amount owed.

Even so, the company swung from a loss of roughly $9 per share in 2023 to slightly past breakeven profits. That is a sizable change even for an REIT where depreciation and valuations play a large part in earnings.

Note that the important debt coverage ratio has been improving the whole fiscal year.

Trophy Properties

There was a discussion on the conference call about trophy properties. These are top-notch properties that are relatively resistant to business level fluctuations. The demand to locate to these properties is high because they are the first to recover from downturns and these properties post the best records.

The results above show the importance of location within good locations.

There are so many office REIT's reporting issues with the properties under management. Even though these properties were decently located, the REIT is struggling. That is clearly not the case with SL Green because a recovery is well underway. That is not the case in much of the industry.

There are clearly some fundamental aspects of this part of the business that all companies have in common. But this company found a way to bounce back quickly and likely more profitably.

Quarterly Results Can Vary

This company has some large clients in different parts of the city. That means that rates vary depending upon the business completed in each quarter. But that rate variance gives the market something to worry about when nothing is wrong.

It only usually takes one significant number for the market to start seeing trouble. As is shown above, the average rent figure is down from the previous quarter. However, that really means nothing by itself because different locations rent for different amounts. The only thing it really means is that another location dominated the rents in the current quarter.

As usual as this appears to be, Mr. Market has long been a chronic worrier. But this type of worry is likely to pass as future quarters are reported.

It was noted that the company is inching closer to the 95% occupancy that should enable some marketing cost savings. The other thing that happens is that occupancy level marks the start of the company regaining some pricing power.

Currently, the company is essentially a “price taker” with prices, terms, and conditions essentially set by the market. However, as vacancies decline, the company will gain some pricing power (and less marketing) to improve profitability and likely the value of the assets as well.

Additionally, there are projects to convert what was office space into residential living space. That will likewise take some office space off the market while entering that same space into a very high-demand market.

Summary

So, the vacancy challenges are being tackled from two angles. Meanwhile, this company passed the 93% occupancy rate in a sector where a lot of competition is dreaming about even approaching a number like that.

Now there is some cause for concern coming from Washington, D.C. All the rapid changes and mercurial remarks cause a lot of uncertainty that is counter to the atmosphere that business in general will do well in. Should it continue, that uproar in the Capitol is likely to cause a general economic downturn that could have far-reaching concerns beyond this company.

But SL Green has proved that even compared to other office REIT's, it is in a far superior position to much of the industry. This reiterates the strong buy investment consideration for this investment idea. Should the worst happen, this is likely to be the company that recovers first (and it likely recovers with superior profits as well).

This company has broken superior locations into parts to find a series of far superior locations that have led to an outperformance, even though this is not one of the more diversified REIT's that I follow. Rather than diversify, this is a management that finds a superior location and specializes. So far, this strategy is working to the advantage of shareholders.

Risks

Any REIT could be stuck if the market changes its mind to go to another location it deems superior. To some extent, management reduces this risk with its new “asset lite” strategy. But the risk of an excellent location changing as consumer tastes (or business conditions) change remains.

A management like this one that has found a superior strategy is often a harder management to replace when there is a loss of key personnel services.

The recovery in New York City can be delayed or even stopped in its tracks by events beyond management's control (like the current upheaval in Washington, D. C.).

Rising interest rates can be a threat to this industry. However, such interest rate rises are likely to be accompanied by inflation, which would allow the recovery of additional costs.

data.title: Nurix Therapeutics: A Story To Monitor
data.description:
Shares of protein degrader (and elevator) concern Nurix Therapeutics, Inc. (NASDAQ:NRIX) have fallen by roughly 40% after a recent spate of conference presentations left investors with few data catalysts in 2025. Data from its Bruton’s tyrosine kinase degrader NX-5948’s Phase 1 CLL study were robust enough to proceed toward pivotal trial(s) for potential accelerated approval that should initiate in 2025. With its NX-5948 neck-and-neck against BeiGene’s (ONC) BGB-16673, an $8.7 billion domestic market opportunity in CLL, and an operating runway to YE26, Nurix merited further investigation. An analysis follows below.

Company Overview:

Nurix Therapeutics, Inc. is a San Francisco based early clinical-stage biotechnology concern focused on leveraging its expertise in cellular protein modulation to develop therapies against oncology and inflammatory targets. The company is currently advancing three clinical programs in Phase 1 studies, including two protein degraders treating multiple blood cancer targets and one protein elevator to remedy solid tumors and lymphomas. It has also entered into several design and discovery collaborations for its technologies. Nurix commenced operations in 2009 and went public in 2020, raising net proceeds of $218.1 million at $19 per share. The stock trades around $17.50 a share and sports a market capitalization of just over $1.3 billion.

DELigase Platform

More specifically, the company’s proficiency is in E3 ligases, a broad class of enzymes that perform protein modulation. Its DELigase discovery platform is designed to identify candidates from its proprietary DNA-encoded libraries (“DELS”) of over five billion unique structures that either inhibit or harness E3 ligases’ natural functions within the ubiquitin-proteasome system to either increase or decrease cellular protein levels. This modulation is accomplished allosterically (i.e., through binding) to reshape a specific E3 ligase that in turn tags the relevant protein by adding a molecular mark called ubiquitin, which signals the cell’s proteasome to degrade or elevate it.

On the degradation side, this approach follows the precept that it is better to eliminate the protein in question than to inhibit it with monoclonal antibodies. There are two other methods of protein elimination: CRISPR and RNA interfering/antisense oligonucleotides. However, Nurix believes its protein degraders are superior as they are orally available – thus providing systemic distribution – easily manufactured at scale, and (unlike CRISPR) reversible.

Protein degraders have already been validated with the approval of Bristol-Myers Squibb’s (BMY) blood cancer therapy Revlimid (lenalidomide). It reprograms E3 ligase receptor cereblon to effectuate protein destruction, representing an improvement upon its more controversial and heretofore mysterious – at least until its true mechanism of action was discovered in 2010 – predecessor thalidomide. As such, a slew of biopharmas, including but not limited to Bristol-Myers, Novartis (NVS), Kymera Therapeutics (KYMR), Monta Rosa Therapeutics (GLUE), C4 Therapeutics (CCCC), and eight privately held concerns are pursuing protein degrader (a.k.a., molecular glue degrader or proteolysis-targeting chimera) programs in the clinic and preclinic.

Pipeline

Nurix’s first two degraders target Bruton’s tyrosine kinase (BTK), a B-cell signaling protein, for the treatment of relapsed or refractory B-cell malignancies and potentially autoimmune diseases. BTK is a validated target with four inhibitors currently on the market, including Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and AbbVie’s (ABBV) Imbruvica (ibrutinib), AstraZeneca’s (AZN) Calquence (acalabrutinib), BeiGene’s (ONC) Brukinsa (zanubrutinib), and Eli Lilly’s (LLY) Jaypirca (pirtobrutinib), which collectively comprised an $8.7 billion in 2023 global sales.

The first three BTK inhibitors (BTKi) covalently bind to cysteine C481 of the BTK protein and irreversibly inhibit BTK but are dogged by off-target binding – leading to adverse events – and acquired resistance due to mutations of C481. Jaypirca binds BTK noncovalently, but is still subject to acquired resistance.

By contrast, Nurix’s BTK degraders do not require covalent and prolonged binding to BTK to trigger degradation, allowing them to affect multiple target BTK molecules sequentially, increasing the likelihood of preventing off-target toxicities and acquired resistance.

NX-5948. Nurix’s lead BTK degrader candidate is NX-5948. It is undergoing evaluation in a dose escalation and dose expansion Phase 1a/1b trial for the treatment of 120 heavily pre-treated (median prior lines = 4) patients with various B-cell malignancies, including chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), small lymphocytic lymphoma (SLL), non-Hodgkin’s lymphoma (NHL), Waldenstrom’s macroglobulinemia [WM], and marginal zone lymphoma (MZL). It was designed to degrade both wild-type BTK and the C481S variant, with limited or no degradation of cereblon neosubstrates. To date, NX-5948 has produced an objective response rate {ORR} of 84% at 16 weeks in the eligible-patient CLL cohort (n=38) with median duration of response not yet reached, as well as a 78% ORR in a nine-patient WM cohort. Owing to these data, which were further detailed at several scientific conferences in 4Q24, Nurix is prepping for the initiation of pivotal trial(s) in post-BTKi/post-BCL2i CLL patients, as well as a monotherapy and in combination with other approved agents in first-line (1L) or 2L settings in a Phase 3 study. Management confirmed in late January that this effort will begin before the end of 2025.

If ultimately approved for post-BTKi/post-BCCL2i CLL (3L/4L) – for which it has received Fast Track and PRIME designations from the FDA and EMA, respectively – NX-5948 would enter a domestic treatment market encompassing ~7,600 patients annually. The annual incidence of 1L patients in the U.S. totals ~19,200.

The candidate has also received Fast Track designation for the treatment of relapsed/refractory WM after at least two lines of therapy, including a BTK inhibitor.

NX-2127. The company’s other BTK degrader (NX-2127) is similar to NX-5948, except that it is also designed to degrade the immunomodulatory substrates of Ikaros and Aiolos. Like NX-5948, it is being assessed in a Phase 1a/1b trial for the treatment of heavily pre-treated B-cell malignancies. However, after demonstrating two complete responses (CRS) in the NHL cohort (n=17) and a 41% ORR in the CLL cohort (n=27) as of a September 15, 2023, cutoff date, the study was placed on a partial clinical hold. This was after Nurix informed the FDA that it was transitioning to an improved manufacturing process. That hold was lifted in March 2024 with enrollment continuing in August 2024 with data anticipated sometime in 2025.

The safety profile of both BTK degraders to date can be characterized as manageable and predictable.

NX-1607. The company’s other clinical program is NX-1607, an oral inhibitor of Casitas B lymphoma-proto oncogene B (CBL-B), which is an E3 ligase expressed in immune cell lineages that functions as an intracellular orchestrator of the immune response by negatively regulating T-cell activation. As such, NX-1607 is designed to promote the activation of T cells, as well as NK and dendritic cells. The potentially first-in-class protein elevator is undergoing Phase 1a/1b study as both a monotherapy and in combination with chemotherapy paclitaxel in the treatment of multiple solid tumors and diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL). Nurix intends to provide a program update in early 2025.

Collaborations

The company’s extensive library of potential therapies has attracted multiple design and discovery collaborations, most notably a September 2023 partnership with Seagen (now part of Pfizer (PFE)) to develop a suite of targeted protein degraders that will become part of a new class of medicine known as degrader-antibody-conjugates. As part of the agreement, Nurix received $60 million upfront and is eligible to collect milestones totaling $3.4 billion and mid-single to low double-digit royalties, with Pfizer handling the development costs. Nurix has the option to opt-in on the development costs of two programs in exchange for receiving a 50/50 profit share in the U.S.

The company also has two older collaborations (both dating back to 2019) with Gilead (GILD) and Sanofi (SNY). The former involves five programs, with Nurix having the option to opt-into two U.S. profit shares, subject to one Gilead veto. Under the agreement, Nurix has received upfront cash, research milestones, opt-in, and research fees totaling $130 million and is eligible to collect another $1.8 billion in milestones and fees, as well as mid-single to low double-digit royalties. Gilead has opted-into one program to date.

The partnership with Sanofi has been revised several times and now extends to five programs, with Nurix allowed to opt-into two domestic profit shares. To date, Nurix has received upfront considerations and research milestones totaling $90 million and is eligible to collect another $2.0 billion in milestones and fees, as well as mid-single to low double-digit royalties. Sanofi and Nurix are moving forward on a degrader of STAT6 (signal traducer and activator of transcription 6), a key target in type 2 inflammation.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

Owing in part to the capital amassed from its collaborations, proceeds from its IPO and a follow-on secondary, the company’s balance sheet reflected cash and investments totaling $609.6 million as of the end of fiscal 2024 year. The company received some $54.5 million in various forms of collaboration payments in FY2024 and $77 million in FY2023. Nurix had an overall net loss of just under $195 million in FY2024.

Owing to its robust early-stage data in heavily pre-treated patients, the Street is very bullish on NX-5948’s prospects. Since late January, 11 analyst firms, including Barclays, J.P. Morgan and Piper Sandler have assigned/reissued Buy ratings on NRIX. Price targets proffered range from $27 to $41 a share. This is against a single hold recommendation from Morgan Stanley ($17 price target). On average, they anticipate NX-5948 to hit the market in late 2027/early 2028.

Verdict:

BTK degraders are the first new class of small molecules to enter the B-cell malignancy space since inhibitors, and have become popular in Big Pharma circles. As such, there is plenty of clinical competition, most notably from Beigene, whose BTK degrader BGB-16673 has demonstrated very similar results to NX-5948 and will enter a 2L+ CLL Phase 3 trial in 1H25. Given their respective efficacy data to date and the size of the domestic CLL market, there would appear to be enough room for two FDA-approved BTK degraders. Furthermore, NX-1607 is being challenged by privately held HotSpot Therapeutics’ own CBL-B inhibitor, HST-1011. That said, with Nurix deep pockets and access to the public markets, the bet here is that NX-1607 will pull ahead, assuming advancement-worthy efficacy, safety, and tolerability.

Normally, it’s challenging to get excited about a concern featuring three early-stage assets. That said, with the results from NX-5948 putting it on an accelerated approval path that should start with trial(s) in 2025 and optionality on its intriguing NX-1607 asset, the recent 34% pullback from its stock’s 2024 high of $29.56 set in November should make Nurix worthy of investment.

However, with Phase 1 data recently updated and NX-5948 only expected to enter pivotal trial(s) in 2025, no timeline on NX-1607 data and a “sometime in 2025” expectation on data from a likely inferior – at least to NX-5948 – NX-2217, Nurix will have a relative dearth of needle-moving data catalysts in 2025. It will likely need to return to the capital markets around YE25 (save a new collaboration).

As such, the retreat in Nurix Therapeutics, Inc. shares could continue through no fault of its own. At or below $15, representing a net $700 million market cap net of net cash, is where Nurix becomes attractive for a small investment by aggressive investors.

data.title: Tracking Stanley Druckenmiller's Duquesne Family Office Portfolio - Q4 2024 Update
data.description:
This article is part of a series that provides an ongoing analysis of the changes made to Duquesne Family Office’s 13F stock portfolio on a quarterly basis. It is based on Stanley Druckenmiller’s regulatory 13F Form filed on 02/14/2025. The 13F portfolio value increased from $2.95B to $3.72B this quarter. The holdings are concentrated with recent 13F reports showing around 75 positions, many of which are very small. There are 36 securities that are significantly large (more than ~0.5% of the portfolio each) and they are the focus of this article. The largest five stakes are Natera, Coherent, Woodward, Teva Pharma, and Coupang. They add up to ~39% of the portfolio. Please visit our Tracking Stanley Druckenmiller’s Duquesne Portfolio series to get an idea of their investment philosophy and our previous update for the fund’s moves during Q3 2024.

Stanley Druckenmiller started the family office in Q4 2011 after closing his hedge fund Duquesne Capital in 2010. Prior to that, he managed George Soros's Quantum Fund between 1988 and 2000. He follows a trend-following trading style that is similar to George Soros. To know more about Druckenmiller's trading style check out Trend Following: Learn to Make Millions in Up or Down Markets.

Note: – Last year, Druckenmiller said he invested in the top-five Argentine ADRs. The top Argentine ADRs are YPF SA (YPF), Cresud (CRESY), IRSA (IRS), Grupo Financiero Galicia (GGAL), and Edenor (EDN).

data.title: Energy Transfer: The Power Story Starting To Play Out
data.description:
Energy Transfer Q4 2024 Update:

Energy Transfer LP (NYSE:ET) reported earnings last week that were largely in line with expectations. EBITDA came in at $3.88 billion, 8% above last year's fourth quarter, and finished the year at $15.5 billion. Cash flow conversion was high with distributable cash flow at $1.98 billion for the quarter after growth capex of $1.22 billion and maintenance capex at $309 million. The results were balanced across segments, where volumes were up everywhere.

The company also issued 2025 guidance for EBITDA ($16.1 billion to $16.5 billion) and capital expenditure ($5 billion growth and maintenance $1 billion). These numbers are larger than Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), which makes sense given how much more aggressive ET has been on the acquisition front over the past few years.

New Growth Initiatives:

Like EPD, ET has discussed how its existing assets (particularly natural gas pipes) could be leveraged in AI's quenchless thirst for power. A day before the earnings report, the company announced a long-term agreement with Cloudburst Data Centers, whereby ET will deliver natural gas from its Oasis Pipeline to a to-be-built data center in central Texas. Instead of ordinary growth deals where the company generally spending five dollars of capital expenditure for every dollar of EBITDA, this deal will require little capital expenditure. In this case, the customer will come to ET. This is a game changer for ET, and frankly, any midstream company with excess capacity on its network. This deal is the first of what, I believe, will be many.

Two other deals were also interesting. The company will build the Hugh Brinson pipeline, an intrastate pipe from the Permian Basin to the Gulf Coast trading hubs. These signal continued confidence in Permian gas production. The company also signed a sale and purchase agreement with Chevron to supply two million tonnes of LNG to Chevron's LNG project in Lake Charles, LA. This is a twenty-year deal, and the type of contract with an investment grade counterparty that makes ET's numbers so stable far into the future.

Valuation:

ET had its best performance in many years in 2024. The units appreciated about 41.5% and distributions added a little over 9% to the total return. This “boring” pipeline co-delivered over 50% total return for investors. While I don't expect that to repeat, I have no interest in selling as the valuation remains reasonable as does the tax-efficient yield.

Market Cap (@$20) $68.59 billion
Preferred 3.892 billion
Minority Interest 11.358 billion
Debt 60. 065 billion
Cash 299 million
Enterprise Value $143.6 billion
EV/EBITDA ($16.35 billion, midpt) 8.7x
Current 6.5%Distribution Yield 6.5%

Risk:

The trading risk here is that oil prices fall dramatically. While that would not impact ET much operationally, MLPs often trade with oil prices. They have decoupled somewhat recently, one-year correlation of .28 vs. a five-year correlation of .50, I wouldn't be surprised to see these units fall if West Texas Intermediate prices (CL1:COM) fell to the low $60's or below.

Given the excitement surrounding AI and data centers specifically, a bursting of the AI bubble and less power needs for data centers would also likely hurt ET units.

Conclusion:

Since this company has reduced its leverage to truly investment-grade credit metrics and shown steady growth spending discipline, I have considered it a core holding for investors looking for tax-efficient income and steady growth. Even after a big run, the units offer compelling value and yield with a growth component. The units just broke through a five-year range of below $16 in 2024. I don't expect a repeat of recent returns, but steady state yield and growth prospects are healthy enough to continue holding.

data.title: Athene Holding: A 7.1% Yielding, Investment-Grade Addition To Our Portfolio
data.description:
Co-authored by Arbitrage Trader.

Overview

In our recent articles, we are showing you the following chart:

This is essential to our idea that the current equity is overvalued. It's time for us to reorientate ourselves and relocate our portfolio to a heavier fixed-income exposure. The fixed-income securities are inherently safer investments than equity, and when offering a higher yield is an opportunity hard for us to pass.

The investment idea we want to present to your attention is the Athene Holding Ltd. Rate Reset Junior Subordinated Debentures due 03/30/2064 (NYSE:ATHS), issued by Athene Holding Ltd., a subsidiary of Apollo Global Management, Inc. (NYSE:APO).

The Issue

The prospectus contains all the relevant information about the security. Here, we will provide only the most important metrics used in the evaluation process.

ATHS will pay a 7.25% annual interest in the form of $0.4531 quarterly distributions until its first possible redemption date on 3/30/2029. After that, the annual interest will be equal to the five-year US Treasury rate plus a fixed spread of 2.986%, resetting every five years. The stated maturity date for the security is 3/30/2064. ATHS was credit-rated BBB by S&P, Baa2 by Moody's, and BBB- by Fitch at the date of its IPO.

The daily price chart of the issue looks like this:

Currently, ATHS is trading at a $25.54 price with a Yield-To-Call metric of 7.09%. If not called, the bond will reset its interest on 3/30/2029 and every five years after that with the 2.986% spread over the five-year Treasuries at that time. Given the fact that there's no way for us to know what the forward yield curve in general and the five-year Treasuries, in particular, will look like for such a long period as 2064 presents, we cannot give a fair estimation of the yield-to-maturity metric for ATHS.

The forward yield curve depicted above gives us an expected value of 4.74% for the five-year Treasury yield at the end of March 2029, when the first call date/interest adjustment for ATHS will be. Based on this, if the security is not redeemed, its annual interest will be adjusted to 7.73%, or around 0.50% higher than the fixed rate of 7.25% at the moment. At the moment, the expectation for the five-year Treasury yield in 2034 is to be higher than at the reset date in 2029. The expected trend is clear and should something completely unexpected happen to the yield curve, we anticipate ATHS to trade to its call date as a low-duration debt issue.

The Company

Despite being fully owned by Apollo Global Management since the beginning of 2022, Athene Holding Ltd. issues its independent financial reports, which we can study to determine to what extent the company's financial state indicates some potential trouble for its outstanding debt.

Athene Holding Ltd., a financial services company, specializes in issuing, reinsuring, and acquiring retirement savings products for individuals and institutions in the United States and internationally. It offers fixed indexed, fixed rate, registered index-linked, private placement variable, income riders to fixed, payout, and group annuities. The company also provides life and other products that include life insurance policies assumed through reinsurance transactions and other retail products, including legacy run-off or ceded business and statutory closed blocks. In addition, it issues funding agreements to institutional investors; and offers a suite of services for investment portfolio, including direct investment management, asset allocation, mergers and acquisitions asset diligence, and operational support services, including investment compliance, tax, legal, and risk management support. Athene Holding Ltd. distributes its products through various channels that include retail, flow reinsurance, institutional, and acquisitions and block reinsurance. The company was incorporated in 2008 and is headquartered in West Des Moines, Iowa.

Source: Seeking Alpha.

Distributions

The table contains information about how the net income of Athene Holding has been distributed in the company for the last five quarters with SEC filings, and also the Interest Payments for the same periods. The trailing twelve months' $473 million in interest payments represent around 9% of the $5.2 billion of net income attributable to the common shareholders and only 6% of the $7.6 billion total net income for the same time frame. If the company remains in its current state of operations, its creditors should feel safe enough about their interest payments, as the cushion for them is large enough. Moreover - there was not one of the last five quarters, in which the net income could not cover the interest payments, and that shows consistency of the good kind.

Capital Structure

Below is Athene Holding's capital structure as of the third quarter report filing for 2024. The capital structure for the previous four quarters is presented, too, for the sake of comparison in the short term.

As of Q3 '24, Athene Holding Ltd. had a total debt of $5.7 billion, which was senior to the preferred equity of $3.2 billion. The common equity of $14.3 billion is the lowest position in the capital structure. We can calculate the debt-to-equity ratio at 0.33. This means that as of the last quarterly filing, Athene has enough equity to cover three times its outstanding debt. This is what we want to see in a company's capital structure when we consider investing in some of its debt issues. ATHS is a junior subordinated debenture, meaning it is with the least priority of all the outstanding debt issues of the company. Nevertheless, the equity cushion is large enough so even the junior debt of the company is well covered.

Credit Rating

This is the beginning of the credit action commentary in which Fitch Ratings affirms the 'A-' Long-Term Issuer Default Rating of Athene Holding Ltd. with a Stable Outlook. This is what the summary of the credit scores of the big rating agencies looks like for Athene:

Athene Holding Ltd.'s OTC bond issues

Athene Holding has quite several OTC bond issues, with various credit ratings and maturity dates. Above we are showing the yield curve of the company's OTC debt issues Baa1-rated by Moody's as they are the ones closest to the Baa2-rated listed issue ATHS. The issues with the shortest duration from this set trade with yields-to-worst of around 5.2%. Keeping in mind that they are a credit notch higher than ATHS, we can assess that the exchange-listed security will be fairly priced to its OTC brothers if it has a YTC of around 5.5% or a price of $27.

Athene Holding Ltd.'s exchange-listed securities

Besides ATHS Athene Holding has five more issues listed on the exchange. However, all of them are preferred stocks, so not exactly the most suitable comparisons to the debt issue we are looking into.

Peers Comparison

Insurance Sector

The chart below contains the exchange-listed debt issues from the Insurance sector, trading above par value before their respective call date, that we deem comparable by their yield-to-call metric. The issues are investment-grade rated by at least one of the three big rating agencies.

The same securities with all the relevant metrics:

Debt issues trading above par

The chart below contains the exchange-listed debt issues from all the sectors, trading above par value before their respective call date, that we deem comparable by their yield-to-call metric. Again, the issues are investment-grade rated by at least one of the three big rating agencies.

The same securities with all the relevant metrics:

Optional Redemption

We may elect to redeem the debentures:

In whole, but not in part, at any time prior to the First Reset Date, within 90 days of the occurrence of a Tax Event (as defined in "Description of the Junior Subordinated Debentures-Optional Redemption"), at a redemption price equal to the principal amount plus any accrued and unpaid interest thereon (including compounded interest, if any) to, but excluding, the date of redemption;

In whole, but not in part, at any time prior to the First Reset Date, within 90 days of the occurrence of a Tax Event (as defined in "Description of the Junior Subordinated Debentures-Optional Redemption"), at a redemption price equal to the principal amount plus any accrued and unpaid interest thereon (including compounded interest, if any) to, but excluding, the date of redemption;

In whole, but not in part, at any time prior to the First Reset Date, within 90 days of the occurrence of a Rating Agency Event (as defined in "Description of the Junior Subordinated Debentures-Optional Redemption"), at a redemption price equal to 102% of the principal amount plus any accrued and unpaid interest thereon (including compounded interest, if any) to, but excluding, the date of redemption.

Source: ATHS Prospectus

Conclusion

ATHS is a Fixed-Rate Reset junior subordinated debenture of Athene Holding, and based on the five-year Treasury forward yield and its current pricing, we expect it to trade based on its yield-to-call metric as a short-term duration vehicle. The issue is relatively undervalued to the short-term OTC debt issues of the company and exchange-traded peers.

Athene Holding Ltd.'s strong financial state and low-leveraged capital structure raise the attractiveness of its debt issues. However, the fact that the common share is not listed can be viewed as a problem by some, as we cannot check how the market evaluates it.

Returns or yields for the fund would have been lower if a portion of the management fee had not been waived. Review the annual or semiannual report for the most current information.

Periods greater than one year have been annualized.

Market Review

Munis retreated. Alongside rising Treasury yields and persistent inflation, U.S. bonds broadly declined in the quarter. However, municipal bonds(munis) generally fared better than the taxable fixed-income market. A third-quarter rally, aided by the September launch of the Federal Reserve’s easing campaign, helped most bond sectors, including munis, maintain one-year gains.

Economy remained resilient. The U.S. economy grew 3.1%(annualized) in the third quarter, similar to the second quarter. Other data released in the fourth quarter were generally upbeat. Led by services sector strength, activity in the private sector expanded, while consumer confidence improved, and retail sales increased. However, manufacturing continued to contract, and the job market weakened.

Munis outperformed Treasuries. Municipal bond yields tracked Treasury yields higher during the quarter, but at a slower pace, and most muni-to-Treasury ratios rose. Overall, munis outperformed Treasuries. Additionally, general obligation(GO) bonds fared better than revenue bonds, and shorter-maturity bonds generally outperformed longer-maturity issues.

Supply slowed in the quarter, grew for the year. As expected, fourth-quarter municipal bond supply shrunk compared with the third quarter’s level, as many issuers pushed deals forward to avoid presidential election-triggered volatility. For the year, total muni supply increased 29% compared with 2023, according to Barclays.

Fundamentals remained stable. While municipal credit trends continued to level off, balance sheets for most state and local entities remained fairly robust. Fiscal 2025 budgets for most U.S. states include modest revenue growth and conservative spending plans alongside strong reserve balances and liquidity. Additionally, rating upgrades slightly outpaced downgrades, and defaults remained rare.

High-yield munis outperformed. Muni credit spreads tightened modestly for the quarter. High-yield municipal bonds declined but outperformed investment-grade munis for the quarter. For the year, high-yield munis significantly outperformed their investment-grade peers.

Portfolio Performance Review

Duration detracted. Our longer-than-index duration position weighed on the portfolio’s performance compared with the benchmark as yields rose. Our positioning along the yield curve, which favored short and intermediate maturities, helped offset some negative duration effects.

Sector allocations aided results. Our sector allocations broadly contributed to results. Underweight positions relative to the index in state GO bonds and prerefunded securities and an out-of-index stake in hospitals were top contributors. Those allocations more than offset modest negative effects from underweights in the special tax and toll facilities sectors.

Security selection contributed. Our security choices lifted results compared with the benchmark, primarily in the special tax, water and sewer and local GO bond sectors. Our selections among charter schools and toll facilities weighed on relative performance.

Positioning for the Future

Growth may gather momentum. We expect slower growth in early 2025, as Trump administration initiatives, including new tariffs and government efficiency efforts, may weaken consumer demand and employment. By 2025’s second half, deregulation, onshoring and rekindled animal spirits may reignite growth. As the Trump administration's tax policy unfolds, we’ll monitor the potential muni market effects.

Credit backdrop to moderate. We expect previously strong credit trends to continue moderating. However, healthy reserves and sound liquidity should help mitigate the effects of slowing revenue growth and waning federal stimulus funds. We also expect an increase in state and local governments facing larger projected budget gaps, such as those challenging Chicago and Oakland, California.

Focused on quality. Given our expectations for growth to slow, we are maintaining a bias toward higher-quality securities and a slight duration overweight. As always, stringent security selection guides our process. With valuations tight and political priorities yet to unfold, we’re focused on select sectors, waiting for better opportunities to meaningfully boost credit exposure.

Commentary Glossary

Adjustable-Rate Mortgages(ARMs): Mortgages in which the interest rate is adjusted periodically according to a specific index.

Agency Securities(Agencies): Debt securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Agency MBS: Mortgage-backed securities issued by U.S. government agencies.

Alternative Minimum Tax(AMT): A parallel tax system that was created to keep high income individuals from avoiding taxes through various deductions and exemptions.

Asset-Backed Securities(ABS): A form of securitized debt backed by loan assets such as auto loans, student loans, and credit card debt.

Basis Points(BPs): Used to express percentage values that are carried out to two decimal places. One basis point equals 0.01%.

Breakeven Rate: Yield difference between nominal Treasury notes and TIPS; indicates the market’s expectations for inflation.

Carry-Oriented Currencies: Higher-yielding currencies of countries where interest rates are generally higher than those of countries with lower-yielding currencies. These higher-yielding currencies are targeted for“carry trades,” where investors borrow money in a low-interest rate currency and invest in a higher yielding currency, potentially profiting from the difference in interest rates.

Collateralized Loan Obligations(CLOs): A form of securitized debt, typically backed by pools of corporate loans and their payments.

Collateralized Mortgage Obligations(CMOs): A form of structured, securitized debt derived from mortgage-backed securities, typically backed by pools of residential mortgages and their payments.

Commercial Mortgage-Backed Securities(CMBS): Securities representing ownership in pools of commercial real estate loans used to finance the construction and improvement of income-producing properties such as hotels, shopping centers, and office buildings.

Commercial Paper: Short-term debt issued by corporations to raise cash and to cover current expenses in anticipation of future revenues.

Commodity: Basic raw materials such as precious metals and natural resources.

Consumer Price Index(CPI): Published by the U.S. government, CPI is the most commonly used statistic to measure inflation in the U.S. economy. Core CPI excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile.

Contribution to Duration(CTD): A measure of bond portfolio risk that reflects a bond sector’s contribution to the overall duration of the portfolio.

Corporate Debt: Debt instruments issued by private corporations.

Covered Bonds: Debt securities backed by cash flows from pools of mortgages or public sector loans. The asset pools help secure or“cover” these bonds if the originating financial institution becomes insolvent.

Coupon Interest Rate: The interest rate that is assigned to an interest-paying fixed income security when it is issued.

Credit Analysis: An analysis of the financial strength of the issuer of a security, and the ability of that issuer to provide timely payment of interest and principal.

Credit Default Swap: Credit derivative contracts between two counterparties that can be used to hedge credit risk or speculate on the changes in the credit quality of a corporation or government entity.

Credit Quality: Measures(usually in terms of high or low) the ability of issuers of debt securities to make timely interest and principal payments.

Credit Risk: The risk that the inability of the issuers of debt securities to make payments will cause these securities to decline in value.

Currency Overlay: A financial trading strategy used to separate the management of currency risk from other portfolio strategies. A currency

overlay manager can seek to hedge the risk from adverse movements in exchange rates, and/or attempt to profit from tactical currency views.

Debt Service: The amount of money required within a given period to keep current on required/scheduled repayments of outstanding debt, including interest and principal.

Deflation: A decline in prices for goods, assets and services.

Derivatives: Securities whose performance and/or structure is derived from the performance and/or structure of other assets, interest rates or indexes.

Duration: A measure of the price sensitivity of a fixed income investment to changes in interest rates. The longer the duration, expressed in years, the more a fixed income investment’s price will change when interest rates change.

Emerging Markets(EM) Debt: Debt issued by countries whose economies are considered to be developing or emerging from underdevelopment.

Excess Return: The return of an investment portfolio minus the return of what is considered to be a relatively risk-free asset, such as a U.S. Treasury bill.

Exchange-Traded Fund(ETF): Represents a group of securities but is traded on an exchange like an individual stock.

Federal Funds Rate: An overnight interest rate banks charge each other for loans.

Federal Open Market Committee(FOMC): The committee that sets interest rate and credit policies for the Federal Reserve System, the U.S. central bank.

Federal Reserve(Fed): The U.S. central bank, responsible for monetary policies affecting the U.S. financial system and the economy.

Fundamental Factors: Economic or financial data used in determining asset value.

Futures Contracts(Futures): Agreements to buy or sell a specific amount of a commodity or financial instrument at a particular price on a stipulated future date.

General Obligation(GO) Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the full faith and credit of the issuer, including its taxing power.

Gross Domestic Product(GDP): A measure of the total economic output in goods and services for an economy.

Government-Sponsored Enterprises(GSEs): Privately owned corporations created by Congress to provide funding and help to reduce the cost of capital for certain borrowing sectors of the economy such as homeowners, students and farmers.

Headline Risk: Refers to the risk that a negative news media headline about one security issuer, incident or sector could affect the demand for and pricing of a much wider swath of securities.

Hedge: An investment designed to reduce the risk of an adverse price move in another investment. Often a hedge consists of taking an offsetting position in a related investment, such as a futures contract.

High-Yield(HY) Debt: Fixed income securities with lower credit quality and lower credit ratings. High-yield securities are rated below BBB-.

Hybrid Adjustable-Rate Mortgages(ARMs): Combine the characteristics of fixed-rate and adjustable-rate mortgages, with rates remaining fixed for a set period of time and then adjusting periodically according to a specific index.

Inflation: An economic condition of rising prices for goods, services and assets, or equivalently, a declining value of money. Core inflation excludes food and energy prices, which tend to be volatile. It is the opposite of Deflation.

Inflation-Indexed Securities: Debt securities that offer returns adjusted for inflation. Typically, the principal of inflation-indexed securities is indexed to a widely used inflation measure and adjusted accordingly. Interest payments can be adjusted as well.

Interest Rate Risk: The risk that a fixed income investment’s value will change due to changes in interest rates.

Investment-Grade(IG) Debt: Fixed income securities with relatively high credit quality and credit ratings in the upper ranges of those provided by credit rating services. Using Standard& Poor’s ratings as the benchmark, investment-grade securities are those rated BBB- or higher.

Leverage: The use of financial instruments and/or borrowed capital to increase potential returns or to increase purchasing power.

Long Position: Typical ownership of an asset or investment that gives the owner the right to transfer ownership, the right to any income generated by the asset, and the right to any profits or losses due to value changes.

Long/Short Position: A“market neutral” investing strategy that involves taking long positions in assets that are expected to increase in value and short positions in assets that are expected to decrease in value.

Master Limited Partnerships(MLPs): Publicly traded, generally higher-yielding securities of enterprises that engage in certain businesses, usually pertaining to the use of natural resources.

Mortgage-Backed Securities(MBS): A form of securitized debt that represents ownership in pools of mortgage loans and their payments.

MSCI ACWI(All Country World Index): A free float-adjusted market capitalization-weighted index that is designed to measure the equity market performance of developed and emerging markets.

Municipal Securities: Debt securities typically issued by or on behalf of

U.S. state and local governments, their agencies or authorities to raise money for a variety of public purposes, including financing for state and local governments as well as financing for specific projects and public facilities.

Nominal Yield: The interest rate to par value that the bond issuer promises to pay bond purchasers.

Non-Agency Mortgage-Backed Securities(MBS): MBS are groups of home mortgages that are sold by the issuing banks and then packaged together into"pools" and sold as a single security. Non-agency MBS are issued by private entities, such as financial institutions.

Personal Consumption Expenditures(PCE): This price deflator which comes from the Bureau of Economic Analysis' quarterly report on U.S. gross domestic product is based on a survey of businesses and is intended to capture the price changes in all final goods, no matter the purchaser. Because of its broader scope and certain differences in the methodology used to calculate the PCE price index, the Federal Reserve holds the PCE deflator as its preferred, consistent measure of inflation over time.

Quantitative Easing(QE): A form of monetary policy used by central banks to stimulate economic growth by purchasing domestic government securities to increase the domestic money supply, lower interest rates and encourage investors to make investments in riskier assets such as stocks and high-yield securities.

Real Estate Investment Trusts(REITs): Securities that trade like stocks and invest in real estate through properties or mortgages.

Real Yield: A yield that has been adjusted to remove the effects of inflation.

Revenue Bonds: Municipal bonds that are secured by the net revenues from the project or facility being financed.

S&P 500 Index: The S&P 500® Index is composed of 500 selected common stocks most of which are listed on the New York Stock Exchange. It is not an investment product available for purchase.

Secondary Market: A market where investors make purchases from other investors, rather than from the initial issuers.

Securitized Debt: Debt resulting from aggregating debt instruments into a pool of similar debts, then issuing new securities backed by the pool.

ABS, MBS, and CMOs are common forms of securitized debt.

Short Position: Refers to the sale of an asset borrowed, not owned, by the seller in anticipation of a price decline. If the seller can buy the asset later(to return what was borrowed) at a lower price, a profit results. If the price rises, the borrower/seller suffers a loss.

Sovereign Debt: A country's government-issued debt, priced in its native currency, which can be sold to investors in other countries.

Spreads, Maturity Spreads, Credit Spreads: Measured differences between two interest rates or yields that are being compared with each other. Spreads typically are measured between fixed income securities of the same credit quality, but different maturities(maturity spreads), or of the same maturity, but different credit quality(credit spreads).

Spread Sectors: Non-Treasury debt sectors with securities that usually trade at higher yields than comparable-maturity U.S. Treasury securities. These sectors typically trade at higher yields than Treasuries because they usually have relatively higher credit risk and/or more risk of prepayment.

Spread Widening, Tightening: Changes in spreads that reflect changes in relative value, with spread widening usually indicating relative price depreciation and spread tightening indicating relative price appreciation.

Swaps: Two-party contracts entered into primarily by institutional investors for periods ranging from a few weeks to more than one year. In a standard“swap” transaction, two parties agree to exchange the returns(or differentials in rates of return) earned or realized on particular predetermined investments or instruments.

Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities(TIPS): Inflation-linked debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury.

Technical Factors: Market price behavior, trends, volume, and momentum data used in determining asset value.

U.S. Treasury securities: Debt securities issued by the U.S. Treasury and backed by the direct"full faith and credit" pledge of the U.S. government. Treasury securities include bills(maturing in one year or less), notes(maturing in two to 10 years) and bonds(maturing in more than 10 years). They are generally considered among the highest quality and most liquid securities in the world.

Variable-Rate Demand Notes(VRDNs): Short-term debt securities that track market interest rates using periodic(daily, weekly, monthly, or quarterly) interest rate adjustments.

Weighted Average Life to Maturity: The average time in years to receive the principal repayments.

Yield: A rate of return for bonds and other fixed-income securities. There are several types of yields and yield calculations.

Yield Curve: A line graph showing the yields of fixed income securities from a single sector from a range of different maturities at a single point in time.

Yield to Maturity: A common performance calculation for fixed income securities, which takes into account total annual interest payments, the purchase price, the redemption value, and the amount of time remaining until maturity.

Real Yield to Maturity: Yield to maturity minus any"inflation premium" that had been added/priced in.

Zero-Coupon Securities: Debt securities that are sold at a deep discount then redeemed for their full face value at maturity.

Glossary Sources: American Century Investments, Bloomberg Indices.

American Century Investments Services, Inc., Distributor©2025 American Century Proprietary Holdings, Inc. All rights reserved.

Original Post
